title: Yes please, no thanks
pedagogy: Vocabulary: เอา (mid อ + live เ◌า → mid). Recombination: เอา/ได้ + polite particles via sentence_build (เอาครับ, ไม่เอาค่ะ, ได้ค่ะ); register drills on softening refusals — bare ไม่เอา is blunt toward strangers. Reviews ได้, ไม่, ครับ/ค่ะ and ขอบคุณ from earlier lessons.

exercises.title: เอา runs every market in Thailand
exercises.thaiExample: เอา · ไม่เอา
body: เอา is “take/want” — the transaction word. Vendor holds something up: เอาครับ means “yes, that one”, ไม่เอาค่ะ means “no thanks”.
Notice what makes the refusal polite: the particle. ไม่เอา alone, with the wrong face, is “don't want it!” — with ครับ/ค่ะ it's a soft pass.
The pattern from this whole unit: verb for yes, ไม่ + verb for no, particle for politeness. That's Thai's entire yes/no system.

exercises.meaning: Yes please, I'll take it. (male speaker)
exercises.explanation: Verb + particle — Thai's “yes please”. The verb answers, the particle smiles.
ไม่เอาค่ะ — negation in front, particle at the end. The ค่ะ is what keeps it friendly.

exercises.context: A street vendor offers you a taste of something you don't want. You're a man — decline without offending.
exercises.question: What do you say?

choices.thai: ไม่เอาครับ

exercises.explanation: ไม่เอา is the right words but the bare form lands blunt with a stranger — ครับ softens it into a polite pass. (เอาครับ would buy you the snack.)
ได้ค่ะ — the female “sure, no problem”. You'll say this ten times a day.

exercises.context: Your hotel asks if a 2pm checkout is OK. It is. You're a woman.
exercises.question: How do you agree?
exercises.explanation: Agreeing is a statement → falling ค่ะ. ได้คะ mismatches the particle tone, and ไม่ได้ค่ะ politely refuses the checkout you wanted.
